title: 1.1 Explains the "stuck" VU state as graceful completion behavior tied to gracefulRampDown (e.g., VUs finishing current iteration, reserved until graceful period expires, longer residency than stage targets)
1.2 Distinguishes between scheduled handler and graceful handler VU counts as tracking different metrics (e.g., actively running vs max reserved, 1 VU vs 10 VUs)
1.3 Refutes the false claim that execution segments exceed configured maximum (e.g., explains striping guarantees, segments partition without overlap)
1.4  Addresses the VU buffer leak concern by explaining lifecycle management (e.g., GetPlannedVU/ReturnVU cycle, vuHandle manages acquisition and release)
1.5 Explains how handlers avoid racing through coordination mechanisms (e.g., different step arrays, sequential execution, time-based waiting, priority handling)
1.6 Explains ctrl+c / early termination triggers graceful shutdown sequence (e.g., context cancellation, graceful stop signals, eventual hard stop)
2.1 Avoids attributing the behavior to external factors outside the executor (e.g., OS scheduling jitter, Go runtime quirks, network latency, test script errors)
2.2 Avoids dismissing the user's observations as invalid or suggesting the test configuration is incorrect
